title: FlexibleSearch — Query Language Deep Dive
description: SAP Commerce Cloud's type-aware query language for retrieving Models from the database safely and efficiently.

sections.title: What is FlexibleSearch?
sections.content:
FlexibleSearch is SAP Commerce Cloud's built-in query language. It looks like SQL but operates on types and attributes from the TypeSystem rather than raw database tables and columns. The platform translates FlexibleSearch queries into native SQL at runtime, handling table joins, typecode filtering, and localization automatically.

You never write raw SQL in Commerce Cloud. All data retrieval goes through FlexibleSearchService, which is the Spring bean that executes FlexibleSearch queries and returns typed results.

The key benefits of FlexibleSearch over raw SQL are:

  • Type-awareness — queries reference type codes and attribute qualifiers, not table names and column names
  • Subtype inclusion — querying {Product} automatically includes all subtypes like VariantProduct
  • Localization — localized attributes are resolved for the current session language transparently
  • Portability — queries work regardless of the underlying database (MySQL, MSSQL, Oracle, SAP HANA)
Key mental model: Think of FlexibleSearch as a type-safe view over the database. You write queries in terms of your domain model, and the platform handles the SQL translation. This is why changing a type's deployment table does not break any FlexibleSearch queries that reference it.

sections.title: Basic Query Syntax
sections.content:
The structure of a FlexibleSearch query mirrors SQL but uses curly braces {} to reference types and attributes.

Basic SELECT

SELECT {p:pk} FROM {Product AS p} WHERE {p:code} = ?code

Breaking this down:

  • {Product AS p} — the type to query, aliased as p
  • {p:pk} — the pk attribute on the alias p
  • {p:code} — the code attribute on p
  • ?code — a named query parameter (never concatenate values inline)

Selecting Multiple Attributes

SELECT {p:pk}, {p:code}, {p:name} FROM {Product AS p}
WHERE {p:catalogVersion} = ?catalogVersion

Using Generated Constants

Instead of hardcoding attribute qualifier strings, use the generated constants from Model classes — this makes queries refactor-safe:

// Hardcoded — fragile
"SELECT {pk} FROM {Product} WHERE {code} = ?code"

// Using constants — preferred
"SELECT {" + ItemModel.PK + "} "
+ "FROM {" + ProductModel._TYPECODE + "} "
+ "WHERE {" + ProductModel.CODE + "} = ?code"
Exam tip: The _TYPECODE constant is a special static field generated on every Model class that returns the type's code as a string. Using it prevents typos and keeps queries valid after refactoring. Many exam scenario questions present queries with subtle string errors — knowing the constant pattern helps you spot them.

sections.title: Subtype Filtering — The ! Operator
sections.content:
One of the most frequently tested FlexibleSearch concepts is how subtype inclusion works and how to control it.

Default Behavior — Subtypes Included

When you query a type without any modifier, all subtypes are included in the result:

SELECT {pk} FROM {Product}
-- Returns: Product, ElectronicProduct, VariantProduct,
--          and all other subtypes

The ! Operator — Exclude Subtypes

Appending ! to a type name restricts results to only instances of that exact type, excluding all subtypes:

SELECT {pk} FROM {Product!}
-- Returns: only exact Product instances,
--          NOT ElectronicProduct or VariantProduct

When to Use Each

  • Use without ! when you want all products regardless of subtype — the common case for storefront listing pages
  • Use with ! when you specifically need only the base type — for example, when migrating data and you want to avoid processing subtype records twice

Querying a Specific Subtype

To query only a specific subtype (and its own subtypes), simply use the subtype's code directly:

SELECT {pk} FROM {ElectronicProduct}
-- Returns ElectronicProduct and any types that extend it
Common exam scenario: A query returns more results than expected — products appear duplicated or unrelated subtypes appear. The answer is almost always the ! operator: the query should be targeting {Product!} to exclude subtypes that share the same deployment table.

sections.title: JOINs, ORDER BY, and Pagination
sections.content:
FlexibleSearch supports JOIN operations, sorting, and pagination — all essential for real DAO implementations.

JOINs

Joins follow SQL syntax but use type codes in the FROM clause:

SELECT {p:pk}
FROM {Product AS p
      JOIN CatalogVersion AS cv
      ON {p:catalogVersion} = {cv:pk}}
WHERE {cv:version} = ?version
AND {cv:catalog} = ?catalog

Note that the JOIN is declared inside the FROM clause curly braces. This is a FlexibleSearch-specific syntax difference from standard SQL.

LEFT JOIN

SELECT {p:pk}, {pr:price}
FROM {Product AS p
      LEFT JOIN PriceRow AS pr
      ON {pr:product} = {p:pk}}
WHERE {p:code} = ?code

ORDER BY

SELECT {p:pk} FROM {Product AS p}
WHERE {p:catalogVersion} = ?cv
ORDER BY {p:name} ASC

Pagination via FlexibleSearchQuery

For large result sets, always use pagination through the query object rather than loading everything into memory:

FlexibleSearchQuery query = new FlexibleSearchQuery(
    "SELECT {pk} FROM {Product} WHERE {catalogVersion} = ?cv");
query.addQueryParameter("cv", catalogVersion);
query.setStart(0);      // offset
query.setCount(20);     // page size

SearchResult<ProductModel> result =
    flexibleSearchService.search(query);

List<ProductModel> page = result.getResult();
int total = result.getTotalCount();
Performance rule: Never use SELECT * or load all attributes when you only need PKs. Selecting only {pk} and then loading models via modelService.get(pk) leverages the ModelService cache. Loading full objects in bulk defeats the cache and increases memory pressure.

sections.title: Localization and Relation Queries
sections.content:
Two areas where FlexibleSearch behaves differently from standard SQL: localized attributes and relation navigation.

Localized Attributes

When you reference a localized attribute in a WHERE clause, Commerce Cloud filters using the current session language by default:

SELECT {pk} FROM {Product}
WHERE {name} LIKE ?searchTerm
-- Searches the 'name' attribute in the session's current language

To query a specific language explicitly, use the language qualifier syntax:

SELECT {pk} FROM {Product}
WHERE {name[en]} LIKE ?searchTerm
-- Always searches the English name regardless of session language

Querying Through Relations

For many-to-many relations, you can query through the relation's link type:

SELECT {p:pk}
FROM {Product AS p
      JOIN CategoryProductRelation AS rel
      ON {rel:target} = {p:pk}
      JOIN Category AS c
      ON {rel:source} = {c:pk}}
WHERE {c:code} = ?categoryCode

The relation type code (e.g. CategoryProductRelation) is generated from the relation name in items.xml. You join through the link type just like any other type.

Gotcha: When querying localized attributes without specifying a language, results depend on the session locale. In background processes like CronJobs where no session language is set, localized attribute queries can return unexpected results or null. Always set a session language explicitly in non-interactive contexts.

sections.title: FlexibleSearchService — search() vs searchUnique() vs searchRaw()
sections.content:
The FlexibleSearchService exposes several query execution methods, each suited to different scenarios. Knowing which to use is a regular exam topic.

search()

The standard method for queries that return zero to many results:

SearchResult<ProductModel> result =
    flexibleSearchService.search(query);

List<ProductModel> models = result.getResult();
int totalCount = result.getTotalCount(); // total matching rows
int requestedCount = result.getRequestedCount(); // page size

searchUnique()

For queries that must return exactly one result. Throws if zero or more than one row matches:

// Throws AmbiguousIdentifierException if multiple match
// Throws UnknownIdentifierException if none match
ProductModel product =
    flexibleSearchService.searchUnique(query);

searchRaw()

Returns raw values (not Model objects) for queries that select non-PK fields. Useful for aggregate queries or when you only need scalar values:

FlexibleSearchQuery query = new FlexibleSearchQuery(
    "SELECT {code}, {name} FROM {Product} WHERE {catalogVersion} = ?cv");
query.setResultClassList(Arrays.asList(String.class, String.class));

List<List<Object>> rows =
    flexibleSearchService.search(query).getResult();

Executing FlexibleSearch in HAC

The HAC (Hybris Administration Console) has a FlexibleSearch console under Console → FlexibleSearch. This is invaluable for testing queries during development and is referenced in exam scenarios about debugging data issues.

Summary of when to use each: Use search() for lists, searchUnique() for lookups by unique key (and handle the exceptions), and raw result classes when you need scalar aggregates like counts or sums rather than full Model objects.

questions.text: What does the '!' operator do when appended to a type name in a FlexibleSearch query, e.g. FROM {Product!}?
options: It forces a full table scan and disables index usage for that type
It excludes all subtypes from the result, returning only exact instances of that type
It marks the query as read-only, preventing accidental data modification
It is a syntax error — the '!' operator is not valid in FlexibleSearch
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The ! operator restricts results to exact instances of the named type, excluding all subtypes. Without it, querying {Product} returns Products, VariantProducts, ElectronicProducts, and every other subtype. Use ! when you specifically need only the base type's instances.

questions.text: In FlexibleSearch, query parameters should be concatenated directly into the query string using Java string operations for simplicity.
questions.explanation: False. Parameters must use the ?paramName placeholder syntax and be passed via query.addQueryParameter(). String concatenation is a security risk (equivalent to SQL injection), breaks with special characters, and prevents query plan caching by the platform.

questions.text: A query runs 'SELECT {pk} FROM {Product}'. Which of the following will be included in the results?
options: Only exact Product instances — subtypes are excluded by default
Product instances and all subtypes such as VariantProduct and ElectronicProduct
Only Products from the current catalog version
Only Products whose PK is non-null — nulls are filtered automatically
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. Without the ! operator, FlexibleSearch includes all subtypes of the queried type. {Product} returns every instance of Product and any type that extends it. To restrict to exact Product instances only, use {Product!}.

questions.text: What happens when 'flexibleSearchService.searchUnique()' is called and the query matches two rows?
options: It returns the first matching result and silently discards the second
It returns both results wrapped in a List
It throws an AmbiguousIdentifierException
It throws a FlexibleSearchException with a generic error message
questions.explanation: Option C is correct. searchUnique() enforces that exactly one result is returned. If multiple rows match, it throws AmbiguousIdentifierException. If no rows match, it throws UnknownIdentifierException. Use search() when zero or multiple results are acceptable.

questions.text: In a FlexibleSearch JOIN, the join condition is declared inside the FROM clause curly braces, not in a separate ON clause after the FROM block.
questions.explanation: True. FlexibleSearch JOIN syntax places the entire join definition — including the ON condition — inside the FROM {} block. This differs from standard SQL where JOIN and ON appear after the FROM clause. The pattern is: FROM {TypeA AS a JOIN TypeB AS b ON {a:attr} = {b:pk}}.

questions.text: A developer needs to search products by their English name regardless of the current session language. Which query syntax is correct?
options: WHERE {name} LIKE ?term AND {language} = 'en'
WHERE {name[en]} LIKE ?term
WHERE LANG({name}, 'en') LIKE ?term
WHERE {name:en} LIKE ?term
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The language qualifier syntax for localized attributes in FlexibleSearch is {attribute[languageCode]}. Without specifying a language, the query uses the current session locale, which is unpredictable in background processes.

questions.text: Which FlexibleSearchService method should be used when a query selects scalar values like counts or strings rather than full Model objects?
options: searchUnique(), with the result cast to the expected scalar type
search(), with setResultClassList() defining the expected Java types for each selected column
searchRaw(), which is a separate service method dedicated to non-Model queries
searchScalar(), the platform method specifically designed for aggregate queries
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. For scalar results, use search() with query.setResultClassList() to define the expected Java type for each selected column. The result comes back as List<List<Object>>. There is no separate searchRaw() or searchScalar() method on the service.

questions.text: FlexibleSearch queries are portable across different database vendors (MySQL, MSSQL, SAP HANA) because the platform translates them to native SQL at runtime.
questions.explanation: True. This is one of the primary reasons FlexibleSearch exists. Because queries reference types and attributes rather than database tables and columns, the platform can generate database-specific SQL at runtime. This portability is a deliberate design goal of the Commerce Cloud platform.

questions.text: How should pagination be implemented in a FlexibleSearch DAO to avoid loading all records into memory?
options: Load all results with search() and use Java's List.subList() to extract the needed page
Use query.setStart() and query.setCount() on the FlexibleSearchQuery object before executing
Append 'LIMIT ?count OFFSET ?start' to the query string as in standard SQL
Pagination is not supported in FlexibleSearch — use ABAP reports for large datasets
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. FlexibleSearchQuery exposes setStart() (offset) and setCount() (page size) for server-side pagination. The database applies the limit before returning results, avoiding memory overhead. Never load all rows and slice in Java — this is a common performance bug in Commerce Cloud projects.

questions.text: The _TYPECODE constant on a generated Model class (e.g. ProductModel._TYPECODE) returns the numeric typecode integer used in the database.
questions.explanation: False. _TYPECODE returns the type's code string (e.g. "Product"), not the numeric typecode integer. It is used in FlexibleSearch query strings inside {} brackets. The numeric typecode is a database-level identifier; the code string is what FlexibleSearch and the TypeSystem use at the API level.

questions.text: A CronJob executes a FlexibleSearch query on a localized attribute without specifying a language. What is the likely result?
options: The query always defaults to English regardless of context
The query fails with a LocalizationException because no language is set
The query uses the session language, which may be unset in background processes, causing unpredictable or null results
FlexibleSearch automatically queries all languages and merges the results
questions.explanation: Option C is correct. Without an explicit language qualifier, FlexibleSearch resolves localized attributes using the current session language. In CronJobs and background processes, no session language is set by default, which can produce null values or unexpected behavior. Always set the session language explicitly in non-interactive contexts.

questions.text: What does 'result.getTotalCount()' return on a paginated FlexibleSearchService.search() result?
options: The number of results in the current page only
The total number of rows matching the query across all pages
The total number of types in the TypeSystem matching the FROM clause
The number of results requested via query.setCount()
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. getTotalCount() returns the full count of matching rows in the database, regardless of the page size set via setCount(). getRequestedCount() returns the page size. This distinction matters for building pagination UIs that show total pages.
